questions.question: What are the three elements required to establish a claim in proprietary estoppel?

options.B: A representation or assurance by the owner that the claimant has or will have rights over the land; detrimental reliance by the claimant on that representation; and unconscionability — it would be unconscionable for the owner to resile.
options.C: A written agreement; payment of money; and registration of the interest at the Land Registry.
options.D: Common intention; direct financial contribution; and a claim made within the limitation period.

questions.explanation: Proprietary estoppel arises where three elements are established: (1) the owner of land makes a representation or assurance — express or implied — that the claimant has or will have rights over that land; (2) the claimant relies on that representation to their detriment; and (3) it would be unconscionable for the owner to resile from the representation in light of the claimant's reliance. Where all three elements are established, equity will grant an appropriate remedy.

questions.question: In Pascoe v Turner (1979), what assurance gave rise to a proprietary estoppel claim and what remedy did the court grant?

options.A: A written promise to transfer the property; the court ordered specific performance of the promise.
options.B: The owner told his girlfriend after their relationship ended that the house and everything in it was hers; she spent money improving it in reliance; the court ordered the transfer of the fee simple to her.
options.C: A father promised his son the family farm; the son worked on it unpaid for years; the court awarded a life interest.
options.D: A landlord assured a tenant she could remain indefinitely; the tenant renovated the property; the court granted a licence to occupy.

questions.explanation: In Pascoe v Turner (1979), the owner of a house told his girlfriend after their relationship ended that the house and everything in it was hers. She spent money improving and maintaining the property in reliance on this assurance. The court held that a proprietary estoppel had been established. Given the nature of the assurance (of full ownership) and the detriment suffered, the court ordered the transfer of the fee simple to her — a remedy proportionate to the expectation generated.

questions.question: In Grealish v Murphy (1946), the Irish High Court recognised a proprietary estoppel claim. What type of detriment was acknowledged as sufficient?

options.A: The claimant paid the full purchase price for the property.
options.B: Spending money improving land in reliance on a promise of a share in the property gave rise to an equity in the claimant's favour.
options.C: The claimant gave up a well-paid career to move to the property.
options.D: The claimant registered the property in their own name without the owner's knowledge.

questions.explanation: In Grealish v Murphy (1946), the Irish High Court recognised that spending money on improving land in reliance on a promise of a share in that property gave rise to an equity in the claimant's favour. This is an important Irish authority confirming that proprietary estoppel is well established in Irish law and that financial detriment by way of improvement expenditure on the land in question is a classic form of reliance.

questions.question: What is the proportionality principle established in Jennings v Rice (2002) in relation to the remedy for proprietary estoppel?

options.A: The court must always give full effect to the expectation generated by the representation — the remedy must equal the full value of what was promised.
options.B: The remedy must be proportionate to the claimant's detriment and need not necessarily be equivalent to the full expectation generated by the representation.
options.C: The remedy is limited to financial compensation and may never include a transfer of the property.
options.D: The remedy is fixed by statute and is not subject to judicial discretion.

questions.explanation: In Jennings v Rice (2002), the Court of Appeal held that the court's task is to prevent the unconscionable denial of the claimant's interest — and the remedy must be proportionate to the detriment suffered, not simply satisfying the full expectation in every case. Where there is a significant disproportion between the expectation and the detriment, the remedy may be less than the full interest promised. The court exercises a wide discretion, fashioning the remedy to satisfy the equity in the circumstances.

questions.question: In which context has proprietary estoppel most frequently been applied in Irish case law?

options.A: Commercial property disputes between business partners.
options.B: Farm succession — promises of inheritance made to family members who worked on a farm for years in reliance on those promises.
options.D: Claims by creditors against property transferred to family members.

questions.explanation: In Irish law, proprietary estoppel has most frequently been applied in the context of farm succession — promises of inheritance made to family members, typically children or other relatives, who worked on the family farm for years in reliance on those promises. The promise of inheriting the farm is the representation; the years of work on the farm constitute the detrimental reliance. This is a classic FE-1 examination context for proprietary estoppel.

questions.question: Can the representation founding a proprietary estoppel claim be implied from conduct rather than stated expressly?

options.A: No — the representation must be made in express words directly to the claimant.
options.B: Yes — the representation may be express or implied from conduct, for example allowing the claimant to build a house on the land.
options.C: No — an implied representation cannot create an equity over land.
options.D: Yes — but only where the implied representation is subsequently confirmed in writing.

questions.explanation: The representation or assurance founding a proprietary estoppel claim may be express or implied from conduct. It need not be stated in formal or explicit terms. For example, allowing a person to build a house on land, or consistently treating them as the future owner, may constitute an implied assurance sufficient to ground a proprietary estoppel claim. The question is whether, in all the circumstances, the owner's words or conduct reasonably induced the claimant to believe they had or would acquire rights over the property.

questions.question: A farmer repeatedly tells his nephew over many years that the farm will be his when the farmer dies. In reliance on this promise, the nephew works on the farm without pay for twenty years, declining other career opportunities. The farmer dies and leaves the farm to a charity. What is the nephew's position?

options.A: The nephew has no claim because he was not named in the will and proprietary estoppel cannot override a valid will.
options.B: The nephew has a strong proprietary estoppel claim — the assurance, his detrimental reliance through unpaid work and lost opportunities, and the unconscionability of denying his interest all point to a remedy, potentially including a transfer of the farm.
options.C: The nephew's claim is limited to wages for the work he performed, assessed at the market rate.
options.D: The charity holds the farm subject to a resulting trust in the nephew's favour.

questions.explanation: The nephew has a strong proprietary estoppel claim. All three elements are present: (1) assurance — the farmer's repeated statements that the farm would be the nephew's; (2) detrimental reliance — twenty years of unpaid work and foregone career opportunities; (3) unconscionability — having made consistent promises and received the benefit of the nephew's labour, it would be unconscionable to deny him any interest. The court would fashion an appropriate remedy, potentially including a transfer of the farm, following Grealish v Murphy (1946) and the proportionality principle from Jennings v Rice (2002).

questions.question: What range of remedies may a court grant in a successful proprietary estoppel claim?

options.A: Only a monetary award — the court cannot transfer property or grant an interest in land.
options.B: The court has a wide discretion and may grant a fee simple, a licence, monetary compensation, or an interest in a specified proportion of the property — the remedy is fashioned to satisfy the equity proportionately.
options.C: The court must grant the precise interest promised in the assurance — nothing more and nothing less.
options.D: The court may only grant a remedy equivalent to the claimant's measurable financial expenditure.

questions.explanation: The remedy for proprietary estoppel is entirely discretionary. The court may grant: a fee simple — where the expectation was of full ownership and the detriment was substantial, as in Pascoe v Turner (1979); a licence to occupy; monetary compensation; or a proportionate interest in the property. The court exercises a proportionality analysis — the remedy must be proportionate to the claimant's detriment, not necessarily equal to the full expectation. Gillett v Holt confirms the flexibility of the doctrine.

questions.question: What role does the principle of unconscionability play in proprietary estoppel?

options.A: Unconscionability is irrelevant once assurance and detriment are proved — the court must grant a remedy automatically.
options.B: Unconscionability is the overarching lens through which all three elements are assessed — the court must be satisfied that it would be unconscionable for the owner to resile from the assurance in light of the claimant's reliance.
options.C: Unconscionability is only relevant to the question of remedy, not to the establishment of the claim.
options.D: Unconscionability substitutes for proof of detriment where the claimant has acted reasonably.

questions.explanation: Unconscionability is the overarching lens through which the three elements of proprietary estoppel — representation, detrimental reliance, and unconscionability — are assessed. Even where representation and reliance are established, the court considers whether, in all the circumstances, it would be unconscionable for the representor to deny the claimant's interest. This imports a broad discretion into the doctrine and ensures that it operates to prevent genuine injustice rather than as a technical mechanism.

questions.question: For FE-1 examination purposes, which of the following correctly sets out the key points to address in a proprietary estoppel question?

options.A: Whether the trust satisfies the three certainties; whether the trustees have acted in breach; and whether section 62 relief is available.
options.B: The three elements of proprietary estoppel — representation, detrimental reliance, and unconscionability; the relevant Irish authority (Grealish v Murphy 1946); the proportionality principle from Jennings v Rice (2002); and the range of available remedies.
options.C: Whether the property is registered; whether the claimant has a legal interest; and whether specific performance is available.
options.D: Whether the common intention test from Lloyds Bank v Rosset is satisfied; whether there were direct financial contributions; and whether the beneficial interest has been quantified.

questions.explanation: For FE-1 purposes, a proprietary estoppel question requires: (1) identifying and applying the three elements — representation or assurance (express or implied); detrimental reliance; and unconscionability; (2) citing Irish authority — Grealish v Murphy (1946) — alongside English authorities such as Pascoe v Turner (1979) and Jennings v Rice (2002); (3) applying the proportionality principle from Jennings v Rice to the remedy; and (4) identifying the range of available remedies and advising which is most appropriate on the facts. Farm succession scenarios are the classic FE-1 context.
